senses.definition: to attempt to escape quickly or suddenly from a situation or place

examples.x: When they saw the opportunity, they decided to make a break for the nearby woods to avoid the pursuing enemy.
As soon as the door opened, the cat made a break for the garden.
When the teacher's back was turned, the students made a break for the classroom door.
As the alarm sounded, the thief made a break for the exit with the stolen goods.
When they saw the guards were distracted, they decided to make a break for freedom.

senses.definition: to provide assistance, support, or something valuable to a cause, organization, or effort

examples.x: She wanted to make a contribution to the local animal shelter, so she donated some food and blankets for the animals.
She decided to make a contribution to the local charity to help those in need.
The artist made a contribution to the art exhibition by showcasing their latest masterpiece.
Students were encouraged to make a contribution to the school fundraiser by selling tickets.
Companies often make a contribution to environmental initiatives to promote sustainability.

senses.definition: to find or identify something new or previously unknown, often through research, exploration, or investigation

examples.x: The scientist was excited to make a discovery that could revolutionize the field of medicine.
Through extensive research, historians made a discovery of lost documents from the medieval era.
The detective made a discovery that led to the solving of the cold case.
After years of exploration, the team finally made a discovery of a new species of plant.
Through extensive research, historians made a discovery about an undocumented event in history.

senses.definition: to react with excessive or unnecessary attention or agitation about something

examples.x: She tends to make a fuss about minor issues, which can be quite exhausting.
The child made a fuss when he couldn't find his favorite toy.
He made a fuss over the slightest mistake, causing unnecessary stress for everyone involved.
The celebrity made a fuss about the paparazzi following her everywhere she went.
The dog made a fuss when its owner left the house, whining and barking until they returned.

senses.definition: to regularly or repeatedly do something as part of one's routine or behavior

examples.x: He has made a habit of going for a jog every morning to stay healthy.
She made a habit of going for a jog every morning before work.
The teacher made a habit of arriving early to prepare for the day's lessons.
She made a habit of taking a short break every hour to stretch and relax.
They made a habit of writing in their gratitude journal before going to sleep.

senses.definition: to travel from one place to another, often involving a significant distance

examples.x: We decided to make a journey to the mountains to enjoy a weekend of hiking and relaxation.
They made a journey across the country to visit family for the holidays.
He made a journey to the city to attend a business conference.
The pilgrims made a journey to the holy site to fulfill their religious obligations.
We made a journey by car to the beach for a relaxing vacation.

senses.definition: to take action in order to achieve something

examples.x: No one made a move to help.
They have made a move to expand their business into new markets.
He made a move to apologize for his mistake yesterday.
Right now, he is making a move to negotiate a better deal with the client.
Tomorrow, we will make a move to finalize the contract.

senses.definition: to record or write down something for the purpose of remembering it or referring to it later

examples.x: I always make a note of important dates in my calendar to ensure I don't forget them
Don't forget to make a note of the address before you leave.
He made a note in his journal to remember his goals for the year.

senses.definition: to emphasize, express, or communicate a specific idea or argument

examples.x: During the meeting, she wanted to make a point about the importance of teamwork in achieving our goals.
While they were discussing the project, she made a point to clarify the deadline.
They have made a point to address customer concerns promptly.
He made a point to highlight the benefits of the new policy last week.

senses.definition: to commit to doing something in the future

examples.x: She decided to make a promise to her children that she would always be there for them.
The politician made a promise to improve healthcare access if elected.
While they were discussing their future together, she made a promise to always be there for him.
He made a promise to take her out for dinner last weekend.
They have made a promise to donate a portion of their profits to charity every year.

senses.definition: to ask for something or express a desire for someone to do something

examples.x: He decided to make a request for some extra time to complete the project.
He made a request for his colleagues to help him with the presentation.
He will make a request for permission to take time off next month.
They made a request for changes to be made to the contract.
The teacher made a request for students to turn off their cell phones during class.

senses.definition: to secure a place, seat, or accommodation in advance

examples.x: We called the restaurant to make a reservation for a table for two on Saturday night.
He made a reservation for a rental car for their vacation last summer.
She makes a reservation for two at the restaurant for dinner tonight.
They will make a reservation for a guided tour of the museum during their trip to the city.
They made a reservation for a cabin in the mountains for their anniversary trip.

senses.definition: to take action to support or defend a position or opinion

examples.x: The activists decided to make a stand against environmental pollution by organizing a protest.
She makes a stand for gender equality and women's rights in her community.
The athlete made a stand against doping.
He made a stand against bullying in his school, inspiring others to do the same.
In the face of injustice, people often make a stand to demand change.

senses.definition: to propose a course of action for consideration

examples.x: During the brainstorming session, she was quick to make a suggestion for a new marketing strategy.
Let me make a suggestion: why don't we go out for dinner tonight?
Can I make a suggestion for the next team meeting agenda?
John made a suggestion about improving efficiency in the office.
She made a suggestion to her friend about where to go for their vacation.

senses.definition: to go to a place or location, typically for a short period, to see someone or something

examples.x: We plan to make a visit to the art museum during our trip to the city.
We should make a visit to our grandparents next Sunday.
I'm planning to make a visit to the new cafe downtown.
We made a visit to the park to enjoy the nice weather.
He made a visit to the library to borrow some books for his research.

senses.definition: to express a desire for something to happen, often done before blowing out candles on a birthday cake

examples.x: She closed her eyes and made a wish before blowing out the candles on her birthday cake.
Every year on my birthday, I make a wish for good health and happiness.
She made a wish for success before starting her new job.
It's tradition to make a wish when you see a shooting star.
Don't forget to make a wish when you blow out the candles on your cake.

senses.definition: to set a specific time for a meeting, event, or service

examples.x: I need to make an appointment with my dentist for a check-up.
Let's make an appointment to meet with the client next Tuesday.
She made an appointment with her hairdresser for a haircut and color.
It's important to make an appointment with the doctor for your annual physical.
Don't forget to make an appointment for your pet's vaccination at the veterinary clinic.

senses.definition: to organize something, often involving agreements or preparations

examples.x: Let's make an arrangement for the meeting room before the conference.
They made an arrangement to meet at the coffee shop for lunch.
We need to make an arrangement with the caterer for the event next week.
She made an arrangement with her neighbor to water her plants while she's away.

senses.definition: to try to do something, especially when the outcome is uncertain

examples.x: Despite the difficult conditions, she decided to make an attempt to climb the steep mountain.
I'm going to make an attempt to solve this difficult puzzle.
She made an attempt to learn a new language by practicing every day.
Despite feeling nervous, he made an attempt to speak in front of the large audience.
Before giving up, make an attempt to finish the marathon.

senses.definition: to try to do or accomplish something, particularly something difficult

examples.x: He promised to make an effort to spend more quality time with his family.
She made an effort to understand the complex math problem.
Let's make an effort to finish the project ahead of schedule.
He made an effort to improve his fitness by going to the gym regularly.
We need to make an effort to reduce our carbon footprint.

senses.definition: to successfully free oneself from a place or situation, often involving danger or confinement

examples.x: The prisoners managed to make an escape from the maximum-security prison.
He made an escape from the burning building just in time.
She made an escape from the kidnappers by unlocking the door when they weren't looking.
The prisoners made an escape by digging a tunnel under the prison walls.
The spy made an escape from the enemy's headquarters under the cover of darkness.

senses.definition: to treat a specific case differently from the usual rule or practice

examples.x: The teacher decided to make an exception and allow the student to submit the assignment a day late due to unforeseen circumstances.
The manager decided to make an exception and allow late submissions for the assignment.
Although the policy is strict, they made an exception for medical emergencies.
We don't usually accept returns without a receipt, but I can make an exception this time.
The company policy prohibits remote work, but they made an exception for employees with special needs.

senses.definition: to determine a value through mathematical means

examples.x: He needed to make a calculation to estimate the project's total cost.
She made a calculation to estimate how much paint would be needed for the room.
Let me make a calculation to figure out the total cost of the project.
He made a calculation to find out how much money he would save by switching to a cheaper phone plan.
Let's make a calculation to see how many guests we can accommodate at the party.

senses.definition: to apply a cost for a product, service, or transaction

examples.x: The restaurant will make a charge for the meal on your credit card.
The hotel will make a charge for room service.
The repair shop made a charge for replacing the damaged part.
The online store makes a charge for shipping and handling.
The taxi company makes a charge for waiting time.

senses.definition: to explain something in a way that is easy to understand

examples.x: The teacher made an effort to make the complex scientific concepts clear to her students.
He made the concept clear by providing real-life examples.
The teacher made the instructions clear before starting the assignment.
She made her expectations clear to the team before they began the project.

word_list.pronunciation: mˌeɪk fˈʌn ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to ridicule someone or something in a teasing or humorous manner

examples.x: It's not polite to make fun of others, as it can hurt their feelings.
He made fun of her accent during the presentation.
The children made fun of the new student because of his glasses.
She felt hurt when her coworkers made fun of her ideas.

senses.definition: to simplify a task or situation to make it more manageable

examples.x: She made the recipe easy to follow by providing clear instructions.
The teacher made the math problem easy by explaining it step by step.
We can make the decision easy by considering the pros and cons.

senses.definition: to be understandable in a way that is reasonable

examples.x: Her explanation didn't make sense to me; I couldn't understand why she made that decision.
The instructions were clear and concise, so they made sense to everyone.
It doesn't make sense to drive all the way there when we can just take the bus.
After hearing all the evidence, the verdict made sense to the jury.
It makes sense to save money for emergencies rather than spending it all at once.

senses.definition: to ensure that a system, device, or plan functions as intended

examples.x: The mechanic worked hard to make the engine work again.
Despite the challenges, they managed to make the project work within the budget.
He experimented with different techniques to make the invention work efficiently.

senses.definition: to take steps to confirm if something is correct, safe, or properly arranged

examples.x: Before leaving the house, make sure you turn off all the lights and lock the doors.
Make sure to double-check your work before submitting it.
He made sure to lock the door before going to bed.
Before starting the presentation, make sure all the equipment is working properly.
Make sure to wear a helmet when riding your bike.

senses.definition: to arrange the bedding and pillows of a bed neatly, typically after waking up

examples.x: Every morning, she makes the bed to start her day with a tidy room.
Can you please make the bed before we have guests over?

senses.definition: to set aside a period for a specific activity or purpose despite a busy schedule or other commitments

examples.x: She decided to make time for her hobbies by scheduling them into her daily routine.
Despite his busy work schedule, he always tries to make time for his family.
To maintain a healthy work-life balance, it's crucial to make time for relaxation and self-care.

senses.definition: to intentionally cause problems or difficulties

examples.x: He decided to make trouble at the meeting by constantly interrupting others and disagreeing with every suggestion.
Some individuals enjoy gossiping to make trouble and create conflicts among friends.
It's essential to address any employees who may be attempting to make trouble in the workplace.
The unruly behavior of the group at the concert began to make trouble for security personnel.
Tensions arose when someone tried to make trouble during the peaceful protest.

senses.definition: to create flames for warmth or cooking by using various materials

examples.x: As the temperature dropped, they decided to make a fire to stay warm during their camping trip.
In the wilderness, knowing how to make a fire is essential for both warmth and signaling for help.
During the power outage, they had to make a fire in the fireplace to cook their meals.

senses.definition: to formally agree to be married to someone

examples.x: Against all odds, they decided to make marriage, defying societal norms and embracing their love for each other.

senses.definition: to manage to continue doing something with only few available means or resources

examples.x: When they ran out of ingredients for the recipe, they had to make do with what was in the pantry.
The budget cuts meant the school had to make do with outdated textbooks and limited supplies.
In a small apartment, they had to make do with the available space, using multifunctional furniture.
The hikers had to make do with the food they had left and found creative ways to prepare meals.
During the camping trip, they had to make do with minimal electricity, relying on solar chargers and lanterns.

senses.definition: to do something that makes an already bad situation worse

examples.x: He is making matters worse by refusing to compromise and find a middle ground.
If you try to handle the situation on your own, you will only make matters worse.
She always makes things worse by jumping to conclusions and not listening to others.
The company made matters worse by laying off employees during a recession.
They have made things worse by ignoring the warning signs and not taking action.

senses.definition: to perform an action that is incorrect or unintended, often resulting in an error or oversight

examples.x: I accidentally made a mistake in my calculations, resulting in incorrect results.
She made a mistake by forgetting to save her work, and it was lost when the computer crashed.
Admitting when you make a mistake and taking responsibility is a sign of maturity.

senses.definition: to create disorder or untidiness in a particular area or environment

examples.x: The children made a mess in the living room with their toys scattered everywhere.
Cooking a new recipe often results in making a mess in the kitchen.
Rushing to finish the project made a mess of their workstations with papers and materials strewn about.
Renovating the living room involved moving furniture and temporarily making a mess.
Sometimes, creativity involves making a mess when working on art projects.

senses.definition: to create unwanted, unpleasant, or loud sounds

examples.x: He didn’t want to make a noise and disturb the sleeping baby.

senses.definition: to use military forces and weapons to fight against nations, groups, or individuals

examples.x: The rebels were determined to make war against the oppressive regime.
Throughout history, many nations have made war to expand their territories and influence.
The army general was known for his strategic genius in making war.
The two countries decided to make war on each other after negotiations failed.

senses.definition: to achieve a specific ranking in a competition or event
to arrange or schedule something in advance, such as a ticket. place, or service
